race.name: Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo
race.tagline: Circumnavigate Western Europe's highest peak through three countries. 330 km. 8,000 meters of climbing. France, Switzerland, Italy, and back.

terrain_types: 6+ major cols including Great St. Bernard Pass
vitals.start_time: Very early morning (typically 5:00-5:30 AM due to distance)
vitals.registration: Online via event organizer. Cost: EUR90-120. Registration opens fall/winter prior.
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple feed stations across the 330 km route, at least one per major col. Additional support in valley towns.
vitals.cutoff_time: Strict intermediate cutoffs at key checkpoints. Must maintain pace to avoid elimination. Total time limit approximately 16-17 hours.
distance_options: Grand Tour: ~330 km (205 mi), ~8,000 m gain — the full circumnavigation through 3 countries
Medio: ~200 km shorter loop — France and Switzerland only

climate.primary: Three-country Alpine — extreme altitude and temperature range
climate.description: The route spans valley floors to 2,469m (Great St. Bernard Pass), creating temperature swings of 25-30C in a single ride. Early morning starts in Megeve can be near-freezing (5-10C). Valley sections in Italy and Switzerland warm to 25-30C. Summit temperatures at the Great St. Bernard can be 2-5C with wind. The 15+ hour ride means you experience dawn cold, midday heat, and potentially evening chill. Three-country weather exposure adds unpredictability.
challenges: Pre-dawn start at altitude: 5-10C in Megeve at 5:00 AM
Great St. Bernard summit (2,469m): 2-5C with wind chill below zero
Italian valley sections: potentially 28-32C in afternoon sun
15+ hour exposure means dawn cold, midday heat, and evening chill in a single ride
Three-country weather: conditions can differ dramatically between France, Switzerland, and Italy

terrain.primary: Paved Alpine mountain passes through three countries
terrain.surface: Well-maintained French, Swiss, and Italian Alpine roads. All tarmac throughout. Road quality varies by country — Swiss roads are generally excellent, French D-roads are good, Italian provincial roads are adequate.
features: Col des Montets (1,461m) — opening climb from Megeve toward Chamonix valley
Col de la Forclaz (1,527m, Switzerland) — classic Swiss Alpine pass
Great St. Bernard Pass (2,469m, Switzerland-Italy) — the highest point, historic Alpine crossing since Roman times
Col du Petit Saint-Bernard (2,188m, Italy-France) — border crossing back into France
Cormet de Roselend (1,968m) — classic Tour de France climb, the final major col before the descent to Megeve

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Circumnavigation of Mont-Blanc through three countries in a single day. 330 km with 8,000m of climbing. T2 by score (76). p4 cannot promote into T1.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: length=5 and elevation=5 for the enormous 330 km / 8,000m combination. adventure=5 for the 3-country circumnavigation. logistics=2 reflects the complexity of the 3-country route.

logistics.explanation: The 3-country route creates genuine logistical complexity. You need to carry identity documents and potentially three currencies' worth of cash. Phone roaming across France, Switzerland, and Italy can be problematic. The very early start (5:00 AM) means limited sleep. Megeve itself is accessible — 1 hour from Geneva airport — but the event demands meticulous self-logistics: clothing for 5C starts and 30C midday, nutrition for 15+ hours, and the mental preparation for a dawn-to-dusk mountain marathon.

length.explanation: 330 km in a single day puts this firmly in ultra-distance territory. This is one of the longest single-day cyclosportives in Europe. Most riders spend 14-17 hours on the bike. The distance alone would be a serious challenge on flat roads — combined with 8,000m of climbing, it creates a genuinely extreme test of endurance, nutrition, and mental fortitude. There is no hiding from 330 km.

technicality.explanation: All paved roads throughout three countries. The technical demands are minimal — well-maintained Alpine roads with no surface surprises. The challenge is purely cardiovascular, muscular, and psychological. The descents are fast but straightforward. You want a road bike optimized for climbing comfort over 15+ hours, not technical handling.

elevation.explanation: 8,000 meters of climbing in a single day. This is an extraordinary number — roughly equivalent to climbing from sea level to the summit of Everest. Six or more major cols, each adding 800-1,500m of climbing to the total. The climbing is relentless and distributed throughout the 330 km route. By comparison, La Marmotte's 5,180m is considered extreme — the Tour du Mont-Blanc adds another 3,000m on top of that. The total vertical is staggering.

climate.explanation: The 15+ hour ride window creates extreme climate exposure. You start in pre-dawn cold, climb through morning mist, bake in Italian valley heat, freeze on the Great St. Bernard at 2,469m, and potentially finish in evening chill. The temperature range across a single ride can exceed 30C. Clothing strategy is a puzzle — you need warmth for the dawn start and high passes, but can't carry excessive weight over 330 km. Three-country weather adds the uncertainty of crossing between different weather systems.

altitude.explanation: The Great St. Bernard Pass at 2,469m is one of the highest paved Alpine crossings. The Col du Petit Saint-Bernard (2,188m) and Cormet de Roselend (1,968m) keep the route above 1,500m for extended periods. Multiple hours spent above 2,000m in the latter half of the ride, when fatigue compounds altitude effects, creates genuine physiological challenge. Oxygen reduction at 2,469m is measurable and hits harder on a body already 200+ km into the ride.

adventure.explanation: Circumnavigating Mont-Blanc — Western Europe's highest peak — through three countries in a single day is one of cycling's great adventures. You leave France at dawn, ride through Switzerland, cross into Italy, and return to France before nightfall. The Great St. Bernard Pass has been an Alpine crossing since Roman times. The Cormet de Roselend is a Tour de France classic. The sense of journey — of literally going around a mountain massif that is visible from 100 km away — is unmatched in European sportive cycling.

prestige.explanation: The concept alone carries prestige — circumnavigating Mont-Blanc through three countries in a day. Among ultra-distance cycling enthusiasts, this is a genuine bucket-list event. It lacks the mass-market recognition of La Marmotte or Maratona dles Dolomites, but within the community of riders who seek extreme challenges, the Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo is deeply respected. Finishing 330 km with 8,000m of climbing is an achievement that speaks for itself.

race_quality.explanation: Solid organization for an event of this complexity — managing feed stations, medical support, and cutoff enforcement across three countries is a logistical achievement. Chip timing and classifications provide competitive structure. The smaller field (2,500-3,000) means the organization is stretched thinner than bigger events, and the 3-country logistics add complexity that shows at the margins. But for what it is — a cross-border ultra-distance mountain event — the quality is respectable.

experience.explanation: Dawn in Megeve. Sunrise over the Mont-Blanc massif. Crossing from France into Switzerland. The Great St. Bernard Pass at 2,469m — a Roman trading route, a Napoleonic march, now your cycling route. Descending into Italy's Aosta Valley. The Col du Petit Saint-Bernard back into France. The Cormet de Roselend — one of the most beautiful passes in the Alps — as your final major climb. And then Mont-Blanc, still watching over you, as you descend back to Megeve 15 hours after you left. The experience is epic in the literal sense.

community.explanation: The field is predominantly French with Swiss and Italian riders plus a small international contingent. The ultra-distance nature attracts a specific community — experienced riders who have graduated from shorter sportives. The shared experience of 330 km creates strong bonds, but the field is small enough that you may ride long stretches alone. Post-ride atmosphere is warm but understated compared to bigger events.

field_depth.explanation: The 330 km / 8,000m combination self-selects for a specific type of rider: ultra-endurance athletes with deep climbing experience. The front group is fast, but the competitive depth is limited by the extreme demands of the event. Most of the 2,500-3,000 riders are there to finish, not to race. This is an event defined by completion, not competition.

value.explanation: EUR90-120 for 330 km across three countries with full support is reasonable but not exceptional. The higher entry fee reflects the logistical complexity of cross-border event management. The value equation depends on your perspective — as a single-day cycling experience, it's extraordinary; as a cost-per-kilometer calculation, it's above average for European sportives.

expenses.explanation: Megeve is an upscale French Alpine resort — accommodation is not cheap. Geneva airport is 1 hour away, which is convenient. The 3-country route means potential roaming charges and currency exchanges (Switzerland). Total trip cost from the US is EUR1,000-2,000. Within Europe, the Megeve base makes this a premium-end sportive in terms of total expenses.

biased_opinion.summary: The Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo is not for the majority of cyclists. It is for the minority who have looked at the map, seen that you can ride around Western Europe's highest mountain through three countries in a single day, and thought: yes. 330 km. 8,000 meters. Six major cols. Dawn to dusk. France, Switzerland, Italy, and back to France. The Great St. Bernard Pass at 2,469m is a crossing that Napoleon used to invade Italy — you're doing it on a road bike with 200 km already in your legs. This is not a sportive. This is a geographical statement.
strengths: The concept: circumnavigating Mont-Blanc through three countries in a single day — unmatched in European sportive cycling
8,000m of climbing across 6+ major cols including the Great St. Bernard Pass (2,469m)
The sense of journey: leaving France at dawn, riding through Switzerland and Italy, returning at dusk
330 km of Alpine scenery — Cormet de Roselend, Great St. Bernard, Petit Saint-Bernard — each world-class in its own right
weaknesses: 15+ hour day demands extreme fitness, nutrition discipline, and mental fortitude that most cyclists simply don't have
3-country logistics: identity documents, potential currency issues, phone roaming, varying road conditions
Pre-dawn start (5:00 AM) means sacrificing sleep before the biggest physical effort of your year
Limited field depth — most riders are surviving, not racing, which reduces competitive energy
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If riding around Mont-Blanc through three countries in a single day sounds like the greatest cycling adventure in Europe, it probably is — and you should do it exactly once.

final_verdict.one_liner: Circumnavigate Mont-Blanc through France, Switzerland, and Italy. 330 km. 8,000m of climbing. Dawn to dusk. One mountain. Three countries.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you have deep ultra-endurance fitness and want the most epic single-day cycling adventure in Europe. Yes if riding through three countries around Western Europe's highest peak speaks to your sense of journey. Skip if you haven't completed at least one 200+ km mountain sportive. Skip if the logistics of a 15-hour day across three countries overwhelm rather than excite you.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want similar Alpine prestige with less distance: try La Marmotte (177 km, 5,180m). If you want Italian Dolomites in a more manageable package: try Sportful Dolomiti Race (210 km, 5,000m). If you want ultra-distance without the 3-country complexity: try Haute Route Alps stages.

logistics.airport: Geneva (GVA) — 1 hour to Megeve (best option). Lyon (LYS) — 2.5 hours. Chambery (CMF) — 1.5 hours (limited flights).
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Megeve (start/finish). Upscale Alpine resort with hotels, chalets, and apartments. Book 3-6 months in advance for event week. Megeve is well-appointed but carries resort pricing. Nearby Sallanches or Saint-Gervais offer more affordable alternatives (15-20 min drive).
logistics.food: French Alpine cuisine — fondue, raclette, tartiflette, mountain-fresh dairy. Megeve has excellent restaurants. Pre-ride dinner and nutrition prep are critical for a 15+ hour day. Feed stations provide French sportive fare: bananas, dried fruit, energy bars, cakes, cola, water.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event village in Megeve, day(s) before. Mandatory helmet and bike check typical for ultra-distance events.
logistics.parking: Resort parking in Megeve — limited during event week. Consider shuttle options if staying outside town.

history.origin_story: The idea is elegant in its simplicity: ride around Mont-Blanc. Western Europe's highest peak (4,808m) sits at the junction of France, Switzerland, and Italy, and a perimeter route using existing Alpine passes creates a 330 km loop that touches all three countries. The event was born in the mid-2000s from the tradition of French cyclosportives and the growing appetite for ultra-distance mountain challenges. The route uses passes with deep history — the Great St. Bernard has been an Alpine crossing for over 2,000 years.
notable_moments: The concept itself: circumnavigating Western Europe's highest peak through 3 countries in a single day
Great St. Bernard Pass (2,469m): used by Romans, Napoleon, and now amateur cyclists
Cormet de Roselend: one of the most beautiful cols in the Alps, featured in the Tour de France
The event established the 330 km / 8,000m format as one of Europe's premier ultra-distance challenges
history.reputation: The ultimate single-day Alpine challenge. Not the hardest single climb, not the steepest gradient, but the most ambitious geographical statement in European sportive cycling: around Mont-Blanc in a day.

course_description.character: Epic in scale, relentless in execution. The 330 km route is a circumnavigation — you are literally riding around a mountain massif, and the sense of circling Mont-Blanc drives the entire experience. The six major cols are not stacked as brutally as the Marmotte or Gavia events — there are valley recovery sections between climbs — but the sheer accumulation of climbing over 15+ hours creates a unique kind of attrition. The middle section through Switzerland and Italy, with the Great St. Bernard as the high point, is the psychological crux. The Cormet de Roselend as the final major col, when 260 km are in the legs, is the physical crux.

suffering_zones.label: Megeve to Col des Montets and into Switzerland
suffering_zones.desc: The opening section climbs from Megeve toward Chamonix and over the Col des Montets (1,461m) before crossing into Switzerland. Early morning cold, fresh legs, and the first views of Mont-Blanc's north face. The Col de la Forclaz (1,527m) in Switzerland is the first real test — a classic Swiss Alpine pass that signals the journey has begun.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Col des Montets (1,461m) and Col de la Forclaz (1,527m)

suffering_zones.label: Great St. Bernard Pass
suffering_zones.desc: The high point of the route at 2,469m. A historic Alpine crossing used since Roman times. The long approach from the Swiss side climbs through increasingly barren terrain — the final kilometers above 2,200m are exposed and can be bitterly cold. The summit, with its famous hospice founded by monks in 1050 AD, marks the crossing from Switzerland into Italy. The descent into Italy's Aosta Valley is fast and sweeping.

suffering_zones.label: Col du Petit Saint-Bernard
suffering_zones.desc: The crossing from Italy back into France at 2,188m. By this point, 230+ km are in the legs and the accumulated climbing exceeds 5,000m. The Petit Saint-Bernard is a long, steady climb from the Aosta Valley that grinds rather than attacks. The border crossing back into France feels like the beginning of the return journey, but the hardest single climb still awaits.

suffering_zones.label: Cormet de Roselend
suffering_zones.desc: The final major col at 1,968m, and the physical crux of the event. After 260+ km and 6,500m of climbing, the Cormet de Roselend asks for one more 1,000m+ sustained effort. This is a Tour de France classic — one of the most beautiful cols in the Alps, with the turquoise Lac de Roselend reflecting the mountains. The beauty is real; so is the suffering. The descent back toward Megeve is the reward for the entire day.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: 1,968m summit, Tour de France classic, final major col

course_description.signature_challenge: The 330 km distance with 8,000m of climbing through three countries creates a unique attrition challenge. No single climb is as brutal as the Mortirolo or as iconic as Alpe d'Huez, but the accumulated effect of 6+ cols over 15+ hours, with the Great St. Bernard at 2,469m as the high point after 130+ km, is the defining challenge.

guide_variables.race_location: Megeve, French Alps (circumnavigates Mont-Blanc through France, Switzerland, Italy)
guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Alpine roads through three countries with 6+ major cols
guide_variables.race_weather: Extreme range: pre-dawn cold (5-10C) to midday valley heat (28-32C) to summit cold at 2,469m. 15+ hour exposure.
race_challenges: 8,000m of climbing across 6+ major cols over 330 km
15+ hour ride: dawn-to-dusk time management, nutrition, and pacing
Three-country route: varying weather, road conditions, and logistics
Great St. Bernard at 2,469m after 130+ km — altitude on fatigued legs
Cormet de Roselend at 1,968m after 260+ km — the final major climb on empty reserves

non_negotiables.requirement: Ultra-endurance base: at least three 200+ km rides with 3,000m+ climbing
non_negotiables.why: 330 km with 8,000m of climbing requires a deep ultra-endurance base that cannot be faked. You must have proven you can ride 200+ km in the mountains before attempting 330 km. The final 100 km will expose any endurance gaps ruthlessly.

A 15+ hour ride demands 6,000-8,000 calories of on-bike nutrition. Your stomach must tolerate this — practice eating real food (rice cakes, sandwiches, bananas) alongside gels. Test your plan on your longest training rides. Digestive failure at hour 12 with 80 km still to ride is catastrophic.

non_negotiables.requirement: Clothing strategy for 5C-30C range including fast transitions
non_negotiables.why: You'll experience pre-dawn cold (5C), midday Italian valley heat (30C), and summit wind chill at 2,469m. You need a tested system for managing layers — what to carry, where to stash, how to add/remove at speed. The wrong clothing strategy adds hours to your finishing time.
